title: Ancient Greece & Rome
objective: Students will explore ancient greece & rome concepts including Hidden Voices of Greece and Rome, Troy: Myth or History?, Inclusion and Exclusion in Athens.

lessons.title: Hidden Voices of Greece and Rome

steps.content: Let's explore Hidden Voices of Greece and Rome! Have you ever wondered about this?

Examine the lives of people usually left out of the Greek and Roman story — enslaved people who made up roughly 30% of Athens and powered Rome's economy, women whose lives varied dramatically between Athens (largely confined to the home) and Sparta (physical training, property ownership), and conquered peoples across both empires — and evaluate whose voices are missing from the historical record and why
Let me show you something cool. Describe the daily life of an enslaved person in Athens or Rome using available evidence
Could you tell you about the lives of enslaved people or women in ancient Greece and Rome, and explain why we know less about them than about famous leaders?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Hidden Voices of Greece and Rome: Describe the daily life of an enslaved person in Athens or Rome using available evidence Compare the lives of women in Athens and Sparta, explaining key differences
Let's discover the amazing world of Troy: Myth or History?!

Explore how Heinrich Schliemann's excavation at Hisarlik in modern Turkey raised questions about whether the Trojan War described in Homer's Iliad was historical, partly historical, or entirely mythical — understanding that archaeology and literary sources can support or contradict each other, and that the line between myth and history in the ancient world is often blurred
Time to try this out: Describe Schliemann's excavation at Hisarlik and what he claimed to have found
Could you discuss whether the Trojan War really happened, explaining what archaeology has found at Troy and why the answer isn't simple?

Great job! Today we learned about Troy: Myth or History?. Describe Schliemann's excavation at Hisarlik and what he claimed to have found Explain how archaeological evidence at Troy both supports and complicates Homer's story
Today we're going to learn about Inclusion and Exclusion in Athens. Get ready for an adventure!

Analyse who was included and excluded from Athenian democracy — only free adult male citizens (roughly 30% of adults) could participate, while women, enslaved people (who may have made up a third of the population), and foreign residents (metics) were excluded — and evaluate whether Athens truly deserves the title 'birthplace of democracy' by comparing it with modern representative democracies
Time to try this out: Calculate approximately what fraction of the Athenian population could participate in democracy
Could you explain who was left out of Athenian democracy and make a reasoned argument about whether it really deserves to be called the birthplace of democracy?

Great job! Today we learned about Inclusion and Exclusion in Athens. Calculate approximately what fraction of the Athenian population could participate in democracy List the groups excluded from Athenian democracy and explain why each was excluded
Let's discover the amazing world of Evidence Versus Interpretation!

Distinguish between historical evidence and historical interpretation — evidence is what survived, interpretation is the argument a historian builds from it, and the same evidence can support different arguments
Let's practice together! Development of historical understanding among 9-14 year olds
Does you understand that two historians can look at the same evidence and reach different conclusions — and that history involves argument and judgment, not just facts?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Evidence Versus Interpretation: Development of historical understanding among 9-14 year olds Ways of Seeing: Evidence and Learning in the History Classroom (RRCHNM)
